flashcards.front: What is constructor overloading in C++?
flashcards.back: Constructor overloading is defining multiple constructors in a class with the same name but different parameter lists. The compiler differentiates them based on the number, type, or order of parameters.

flashcards.front: How does the compiler differentiate overloaded constructors?
flashcards.back: The compiler differentiates overloaded constructors by examining the number, type, and order of parameters in the function call to determine which constructor to invoke.
A default constructor is a constructor that can be called with no arguments. It is either defined with no parameters or has all parameters with default values.
A parameterized constructor accepts one or more arguments to initialize data members with specific values provided during object creation.
A copy constructor initializes an object using another object of the same class. Its parameter is passed by reference, typically const reference.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax of a copy constructor?
When is the copy constructor called?
flashcards.back: Copy constructor is called when: (1) passing object by value, (2) returning object by value, (3) initializing one object with another, (4) passing object to function by value.
Constructor chaining is when one constructor calls another constructor of the same class to avoid code duplication. In C++11, this is done using constructor delegation.

flashcards.front: What is an initialization list in constructors?
flashcards.back: An initialization list is used to initialize data members before the constructor body executes. Syntax: Constructor() : member1(val1), member2(val2) { }

flashcards.front: What is a shallow copy vs deep copy?
flashcards.back: Shallow copy copies object members as-is (pointer addresses). Deep copy allocates new memory and copies the actual data pointed to by pointers.
An explicit constructor prevents implicit conversions and copy initialization. It is declared with the 'explicit' keyword before the constructor name.
A private constructor restricts object creation to within the class only. It is used in Singleton design pattern or for factory methods.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between constructor and destructor?
flashcards.back: Constructor has the same name as class, called automatically when object is created, can be overloaded. Destructor has ~ prefix, called automatically when object goes out of scope, cannot be overloaded.

flashcards.front: Why should copy constructor parameter be const reference?
flashcards.back: The parameter must be reference to avoid infinite recursion (pass by value would call copy constructor again). The const ensures the original object is not modified.

flashcards.front: What are default arguments in constructors?
flashcards.back: Default arguments allow skipping some parameters when calling constructor. Syntax: ClassName(int a=0, int b=0). Must be rightmost parameters.
